name: Cova des Pont
starting_point: At km 9.175 of the Ma-4014 road from Porto Cristo to Porto Colom, follow the path through a chained metal gate to Cala Varques, then coast to Cala Falcó and take the track to the cave
clothing: Depends on the time of year
equipment: Neoprene essential to visit the entire cavity
description: Documented since 1884, this cave was connected to the neighboring Cova des Pirata via cave diving in 1998-89, forming a combined 2,105m system. Accessed through a large entrance (~14m diameter) via a stone bridge built during a failed commercial venture, the cave features a grand chamber about 100m long with crystal-clear lakes, impressive columns, and the gated Lake Victoria with its soda straw stalactites reflected in the water. The cave is on private land and requires owner permission to visit.
full_description:
The Cova des Pont, along with the Cova des Pirata, have been documented since 1884, being the first caves in Mallorca to be connected through cave diving by the Welsh group Cwmbran Caving Club (CCC) during the years 1998 and 89. The connection was made after forcing a narrow passage that connected the two caves, increasing the total length to 2,105 m. The two caves are part of the same endokarst system, where collapse processes due to gravitational readjustments of the vaults and walls have caused the separation of both caves by land.

The mouth of the Cova des Pont is of considerable dimensions, approximately 14 meters in diameter, and is concealed by vegetation so that one can walk past it on the path without noticing it. To access it, one must get past a gate locked tight with several padlocks and begin descending via the steps of a stone bridge built when there was an attempt to commercially exploit it by opening it to the public, which gives the cavity its name. The descent is made, as always, among branches of fig trees, a tree present in multiple cavities in the area such as: the Cova de sa Figuera, d'en Bessó, Secret, de sa Figuera, etc.

Once we find ourselves on the first landing, having crossed the bridge and descended a few meters (more or less below the center of the entrance hole to the cave), we will begin the visit to the cavity throughout the non-flooded part of the cave, since to visit it in its entirety we must use wetsuits. We will begin the visit from left to right, starting from the west. To access the first chamber, we will pass behind some ghostly formations, crossing them on the right side and descending until reaching the lateral wall of the cave to find an easy passage and thus avoid the risk of a possible fall. Subsequently, we will need to gain height through a ramp only to descend again until reaching the lake, and then conclude the visit to this gallery, having to return once more to the starting point.

Once back at the landing, we will continue visiting the cave. To proceed, we will descend via the stairs where the steps soon disappear, turning into an earthen path. Then we need to turn slightly to the left and we will immediately access the "great chamber," where if we move along the left side we will soon reach a beautiful lake called the "Carbonate Lake," which, like Lake Victoria, has a sort of natural pier. Noteworthy in this great chamber are at least three columns that are isolated and distributed throughout it — one of them is completely white and another that immediately catches the eye is broken a little above the midpoint. This process is called solifluction, which can cause a column to separate both vertically and horizontally. Also interesting is the topic of light, as from almost any point in the chamber the entrance of natural light can be seen.

We can return to the path to continue our exploration, following its course through a small rise and then beginning the descent while immediately observing an impressive gallery featuring a great chaos of stones piled on top of each other. The dimensions of this chamber are about 100 m in length by about 40 m at the widest point, through which we must move carefully as the ground tends to be very muddy in some spots and there is the possibility of getting hurt. On the right side of the chamber, we will find a first small lake surrounded by concretions forming a charming corner. A little further on, at the end of the chamber on this side, we reach a much larger lake with crystal-clear water. At the back of the chamber, there is a low passage of about 40 cm in height which, once crossed, deposits us in a small chamber with a lake that in turn connects the Cova des Pont with the Cova des Pirata. After leaving this small chamber and returning to the previous one, we can appreciate the backlit effects that make this spot a good place for taking interesting photos using the natural light entering the chamber. As we exit, if we approach the walls on the right side, we will see how it is possible to find water forming a small lake.

Once back, we can continue the visit by accessing Lake Victoria, following the path again and then leaving it via stairs carved into the rock whose first steps are very deteriorated, so care must be taken not to slip on them. Lake Victoria is found behind an oxidized iron bar door closed with an old latch. Every time I cross the door, I wonder what the reason was for isolating this chamber from the rest of the cave by building a partition wall with a window and the access door itself. Noteworthy in this chamber, besides the lake, are a sort of pier that extends into it for several meters and a fairly well-decorated ceiling where soda straw stalactites accumulate, which in turn are reflected in the water when the lake is illuminated.

With the visit to the chamber containing Lake Victoria, we conclude the visit to the cave, so all that remains is to retrace our steps along the path toward the exit.

Right below the entrance stairs, one can access a small chamber where multiple earth collapses can be seen, and which connects to the first chamber described here. This visit can be omitted if desired, as it offers no further interest after everything already seen.

Both the Cova des Pont and the Cova des Pirata and the Cova des Xots are on private land, so permission must be requested from the owners to visit them.

For further reading:

MALLORAL, D. and MATEU, T. 2006: Mallorca, Beauties in the Darkness... Ed. Espelo Mallorca, S. C. Sant Llorenç des Cardassar.

GARCIA, J.; DELGADO, X.; FERRERES, J. 1986: Collection of Cavities of the Island of Mallorca. Exploracions, 10. Barcelona.

TRIAS, M. & MIR, F. (1977): The caves of the Can Frasquet - Cala Varques area. Endins, 4: 21-42. Palma de Mallorca.
